items.q: What happened in the Russian Revolution of 1917?
items.correct: There were two revolutions: February overthrew the Tsar and installed the Provisional Government; October saw the Bolsheviks seize power from that government.
items.misconception: The Bolsheviks overthrew the Tsar in 1917.
items.random_wrong: Lenin personally led the storming of the Winter Palace.
items.why: Students collapse 1917 into one event; the Tsar abdicated in February under liberal pressure, and the Bolsheviks took power eight months later.

items.q: Why did Lenin introduce the NEP in 1921?
items.correct: War Communism had wrecked the economy and caused famine and revolt, so the NEP allowed limited private trade to aid recovery.
items.misconception: The NEP was Lenin abandoning communism entirely.
items.random_wrong: The NEP was a tsarist reform.
items.why: Learners see the NEP as betrayal; Lenin called it a temporary retreat to rebuild the economy while keeping state control of key sectors.

items.correct: It forced concessions like the Duma but did not overthrow the Tsar, who soon clawed back power, leaving grievances unresolved.
items.random_wrong: The 1905 Revolution made Lenin ruler of Russia.
items.why: Students confuse 1905 with 1917; the Tsar survived 1905 and his failure to deliver lasting reform fed the later revolutions.

items.q: Who was the last Tsar of Russia?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Nicholas II. “Alexander III” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What did the February Revolution of 1917 bring about?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: The abdication of Nicholas II. “The Bolsheviks taking power” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Who led the Bolsheviks to power in October 1917?
items.why: It's Lenin. “Stalin” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What was the New Economic Policy of 1921?
items.correct: A partial return to private trade
items.misconception: Total state control of all farms
items.why: Correct answer: A partial return to private trade. “Total state control of all farms” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year did the October Revolution take place?
items.why: Exact answer: 1917 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
